data.title: Japan futures recoil as trade war woes offset off-season tight supply
data.description:
• Japanese rubber futures retreated on Thursday as concerns over global trade conflict weighed on market sentiment, outweighing support from tight supply outlook.

  • The Osaka Exchange (OSE) August rubber contract , was down 4.3 yen, or 1.25%, at 338.9 yen ($2.29) per kg, as of 0221 GMT.

  • The May rubber contract on the Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE) edged up 10 yuan, or 0.06%, to 17,200 yuan ($2,377.07) per metric ton.

  • The most active April butadiene rubber contract on the SHFE (SHBRv1) eased 5 yuan, or 0.04%, to 13,755 yuan ($1,900.96) per metric ton.

  • The U.S. tariff policy caused investors to worry about the outlook for global demand. The macro atmosphere is bearish, weighing on commodity markets, Chinese consultancy Jinlianchuang said in a note.

  • U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to escalate a global trade war with further tariffs on European Union goods, hours after his 25% duties on all U.S. steel and aluminium imports took effect.

  • Trump has also threatened to "substantially increase" tariffs on cars coming into the United States, causing shares of U.S. automakers to slide on Tuesday.

  • Automobile sales could influence the intensity of automobile manufacturing, which involves using rubber-made tyres.

  • Oil prices eased on concerns about the impact of tariff wars on global economic growth.

  • Natural rubber often takes direction from oil prices as it competes for market share with synthetic rubber, which is made from crude oil.

  • Still, natural rubber is in a global low production season, the output of glue is small while the price of raw materials remains high, Jinlianchuang said.

  • Rubber crops usually undergo a season of low production from February to May, before a peak harvesting period that lasts until September.

  • The front-month rubber contract on Singapore Exchange's SICOM platform for March delivery last traded at 195.5 U.S. cents per kg, down 0.9%.

($1 = 148.1700 yen)

($1 = 7.2358 Chinese yuan)

data.title: Proteomics International Laboratories launches PromarkerD predictive test for diabetic kidney disease
data.description: Proteomics International Laboratories has officially launched its unique PromarkerD predictive test for diabetic kidney disease in Australia. PromarkerD is a clinically validated blood test that helps predict the risk of developing chronic diabetic kidney disease (DKD) in type 2 diabetes patients up to four years before clinical symptoms appear. Diabetes is the leading cause of chronic kidney disease globally and the burden is particularly acute in Australia, where more than 1.3 million people are living with the condition. Importance of early diagnosis Managing director Dr Richard Lipscombe said the ability to obtain an early intervention window would enable better patient management while reducing the risk of severe kidney complications, including renal failure and the need for dialysis or transplant. “We are excited to officially launch PromarkerD in Australia, particularly on World Kidney Day, which underscores the importance of early diagnosis and prevention,” Dr Lipscombe said. “DKD is a major public health challenge and PromarkerD offers a highly innovative, accurate and accessible way to identify those most at risk.” “By enabling proactive management, we aim to reduce the personal and financial costs associated with this life-threatening condition.” Key stakeholder discussions The launch supports Proteomics International’s plans to achieve the widespread adoption of PromarkerD across Australia. The company is currently engaging with key stakeholders across local primary care networks, diabetes clinics and professional bodies. It aims to collaborate with health insurers, patient advocacy groups and government bodies to explore potential reimbursement pathways. The test will initially be available through a direct-to-consumer go-to-market route as a prelude to out-licensing to major industry players in the diagnostics sector. Landmark study published Proteomics reported earlier this week that the results from a landmark study into the benefits of PromarkerD had been published in a significant medical journal. The study confirmed that PromarkerD can significantly outperform the current standard-of-care tests in predicting future kidney function decline in type 2 diabetics. The results were published in the peer-reviewed journal Diagnostics in a paper titled “PromarkerD Versus Standard of Care Biochemical Measures for Assessing Future Renal Function Decline in Type 2 Diabetes”. “This peer-reviewed comparison against the standard of care is critical in proving the power of PromarkerD to the medical community,” Dr Lipscombe said. “We want to provide healthcare professionals with the best tools available to combat this debilitating disease.”

data.title: Hong Kong Equities Fall Further
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks fell 69 points or 0.3% to 23,530 in early trading on Thursday, extending losses from the prior session amid declines in tech and consumer stocks.

Market volatility persisted as investors assessed the impact of trade policy developments on the global economy.

Caution prevailed after the conclusion of China's Two Sessions, with economists warning that the country's 5% growth target would be difficult to achieve due to mounting internal and external headwinds.

Beijing’s modest pledges to boost consumption and curb overcapacity failed to ease concerns.

Still, losses were limited as US futures edged higher after slowing US inflation lifted Wall Street from two days of sharp declines.

Meanwhile, the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong recognized the Stock Exchange of Thailand, allowing Bangkok-listed firms to pursue secondary listings.

Among major decliners, Tongcheng Travel Hlds slipped 7.0%, Cathay Pacific Airways dropped 4.6%, while Kuaishou Technology and Meituan lost 2.4% each.

data.title: Palm Oil Rises, Tracking Stronger Palm Olein — Market Talk
data.description: Palm oil rises in early Asian trade, tracking palm olein's gains on the Dalian Commodity Exchange, says David Ng, a trader at Kuala Lumpur-based Iceberg X. CPO futures are also supported by concerns over weak output in coming weeks, he adds. Ng sees support for CPO futures at 4,450 ringgit/ton and resistance at 4,650 ringgit/ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for May delivery rises 36 ringgit to 4,523 ringgit/ton.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Fabtech Technologies Cleanrooms Appoints CEO
data.description: Fabtech Technologies Cleanrooms has appointed Anup Manohar Munshi as chief executive officer and key managerial personnel of the company, effective Wednesday, according to a same-day disclosure to the BSE.

data.title: Shennan Circuit Posts 34% Rise in 2024 Profit; Shares Up 3%
data.description:
Shennan Circuit logged a 34% year-on-year rise in net profit attributable to shareholders to 1.88 billion yuan in 2024 from 1.40 billion yuan the previous year.

The printed circuit board manufacturer's revenue of 17.9 billion yuan, up 32% from 13.5 billion yuan, according to a Thursday filing with the Shenzhen bourse.

Shares of the company rose 3% in recent trade.

data.title: Liberty Files Amended and Restated Unaudited Condensed Interim Consolidated Financial Statements and MD&A
data.description:
VANCOUVER, British Columbia and WILMINGTON, Mass., March 12, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Liberty Defense Holdings Ltd. (“Liberty” or the “Company”) (TSXV: SCAN, OTCQB: LDDFF, FRANKFURT: LD2A), a leading technology provider of AI-based next generation detection solutions for concealed weapons and threats, announces that it has filed amended and restated unaudited condensed interim consolidated financial statements and management's discussion and analysis (“MD&A”) for the three and nine months ended September 30, 2024, and 2023 (the “Amended and Restated Financials”), which amends and restates the previously filed unaudited condensed interim consolidated financial statements and MD&A for the three and nine months ended September 30, 2024, and 2023.

The Amended and Restated Financials and MD&A were filed due to the following changes:

  • Correction of fair value reallocation of 60,000 warrants exercised by $15,275 (Note 11);
  • Correction of residual value of warrants granted by $135,587 (Note 11);
  • Correction of the calculation of fair value of 61,341 restricted shares units exercised by $191,799 (Note 11);
  • Correction of Battelle Patent minimum royalty payment for 2024; accrued for year to date $150,000 added to Intangible assets and $150,000 depreciated and included in cost of goods sold (Note 7);
  • Correction of 465,033 special warrants granted and converted into 465,033 units (Note 11);
  • Reclassification portion of the Parabilis loan from current to non-current (Note 8); and
  • Other immaterial changes.

The MD&A was also modified to include the above changes.

On Behalf of Liberty Defense

Bill Frain

CEO & Director

About Liberty Defense

Liberty Defense (TSXV: SCAN, OTCQB: LDDFF, FRANKFURT: LD2A) provides multi-technology security solutions for concealed weapons detection in high volume foot traffic areas and locations requiring enhanced security such as airports, stadiums, schools, and more. Liberty's HEXWAVE product, for which the company has secured an exclusive license from Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), as well as a technology transfer agreement for patents related to active 3D radar imaging technology, provides discrete, modular, and scalable protection to provide layered, stand-off detection capability of metallic and non-metallic weapons. Liberty has also recently licensed the millimeter wave-based, High-Definition Advanced Imaging Technology (HD-AIT) body scanner and shoe scanner technologies as part of its technology portfolio. Liberty is committed to protecting communities and preserving peace of mind through superior security detection solutions. Learn more: LibertyDefense.com

For further information about Liberty, please contact:

Jay Adelaar, Senior Vice President of Capital Markets

Email: jay@libertydefense.com

Tel: 604-809-2500

data.title: Couche-Tard CEO Miller: No Plan to Close 7-Eleven Stores, Cut Jobs in Japan
Sunnova Prepares Restructuring Talks That Could Include Bankruptcy Filing — WSJ
data.description:
By Jodi Xu Klein and Alexander Gladstone

Sunnova Energy, one of the country's largest residential solar companies, is preparing to engage with creditors to explore options including a potential bankruptcy filing over its roughly $8.5 billion in debt, according to people familiar with the matter.

The company, working with law firm Baker Botts and investment bank JP Morgan, is putting together financial information and could begin negotiating with lenders and bondholders about ways to cut its debt and address near-term maturities, either in or out of bankruptcy court, said the people.

A group of holders of Sunnova's 2026 bonds and convertible notes might propose cutting the company's debtload by swapping some near-term bonds into equity, potentially offering some equity to junior convertible holders, the people said. The group is working with law firm Akin Gump Strauss Hauer & Feld and financial adviser Evercore, The Wall Street Journal previously reported.

Meanwhile, investment bank Ducera and law firm Paul Weiss are putting together a smaller group of debtholders, which is considering a proposal that could wipe out the convertible noteholders as well as equity, the people said.

Houston-based Sunnova, founded in 2012, is one of the main rooftop solar panel installers and financiers in the United States. Like most players in the sector, Sunnova has struggled as demand weakened for rooftop solar panel installations. Competitor SunPower filed for bankruptcy last year as its financial situation deteriorated.

Uncertainties around state and federal tax benefits for solar installations and the potential loss of future loan guarantees offered by the U.S. Department of Energy also are weighing on Sunnova's business outlook.

In 2023, the Energy Department, through the Loan Programs Office, committed to guaranteeing up to $3 billion in debt securities issued by Sunnova to support rooftop solar installations as part of the Biden administration's green financing initiative.

That initiative includes hundreds of billions of dollars in tax credits and loans for clean energy, climate tech startups and other green businesses. However, its future is uncertain as the Trump administration undertakes a sweeping overhaul of federal agencies.

Sunnova's most urgent task is addressing nearly $1 billion of bonds and convertible notes maturing in 2026, sources said.

Last year, the company worked with investment bank Moelis and law firm Kirkland & Ellis to kick-start debt negotiations with creditors holding the debt, the people said. Sunnova later suspended the talks and hired JPMorgan to raise financing.

Earlier this month, Sunnova announced KKR agreed to provide a $185 million loan backed by cash flows from asset-backed securities to boost the company's liquidity. The loan won't be included in a bankruptcy proceeding because it is secured by a separate set of assets, the people said.

Despite the loan, former Sunnova chief executive John Berger said earlier this month the company may not generate enough cash to remain operational due to weakness in the residential solar market. Sunnova reported a net loss of $448 million for 2024, according to its filings with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

Berger, Sunnova's founder, stepped down as CEO this week. He was replaced by Paul Mathews, the company's chief operating officer.

Shares of Sunnova fell and closed at 36 cents on Wednesday. The stock is down 97% over the past six months.

Write to Jodi Xu Klein at jodi.klein@wsj.com and Alexander Gladstone at alexander.gladstone@wsj.com

data.title: Market Chatter: South Korea's Watchdog Imposes KRW83.6 Billion Fine on 13 Global Banks
data.description:
South Korea's financial watchdog, the Financial Supervisory Service, fined 13 global investment banks 83.6 billion won in aggregate for illegal short selling, concluding a 16-month probe, Yonhap News reported Wednesday.

The Futures and Securities Committee, under the Financial Service Commission, carried out the probe, the report said.

The country, which banned short selling in November 2023 after violations, will lift restrictions on all publicly traded firms on March 31. Previously, only the Kospi 200 index and the Kosdaq 150 index stocks were subject to short selling, it said.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Beijing SinoHytec Unveils Acquisition Plan for Hydrogen Energy Firm
data.description:
Beijing SinoHytec signed a framework agreement with Risun Group to acquire its stake in Dingzhou Risun Hydrogen Energy through the issuance of consideration shares.

The company also plans to issue new shares to Risun Group to raise 550 million yuan in additional funds. Risun Group owns a 68.75% stake in Dingzhou Risun Hydrogen Energy.

The final consideration will be determined after an audit and valuation process.

Tradng in SinoHytec's shares, which has been suspended since Feb. 27, will resume on March 13.

data.title: Ionic Rare Earths Says Peer Review of Magnet Recycling Process Shows 61% Lower Carbon Dioxide Emissions
data.description:
Ionic Rare Earths said a peer review of the product carbon footprint study completed by Minviro on its planned magnet recycling plant in Belfast showed up to 61% lower carbon dioxide emissions, according to a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

The peer review showed that compared with the existing rare earth oxide supply chain sourced from mines, the process demonstrated up to 53% reduction in carbon dioxide emissions for didymium oxide and up to 61% reduction in carbon dioxide emissions for dysprosium oxide.

The selection of the oxides as functional units for the analysis reflects the expected product mix from the Belfast commercial plant, per the filing.

The company also said it was launching MagNetZero, a collaborative platform for quantifying and controlling embedded carbon within magnet supply chains.

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0226 GMT - Delfi's growth in 2025 is likely to be dragged by elevated cocoa prices, UOB Kay Hian analysts say in a research report. They lower the stock's target price to S$0.82 from S$0.86, with an unchanged hold rating. Cocoa prices remain much higher than they have historically been owing to persistent supply issues, the analysts note. If the weather continues to be unfavorable in cocoa-producing regions, price increases are likely, they say. The brokerage cuts its 2025-2026 Ebitda margin forecasts for the chocolate confectionery product maker by 1-2 percentage points on expectations selling and distribution costs will remain high due to continued stiff competition. Shares fall 2.0% to S$0.745. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0150 GMT - Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index falls 0.4% to 23499.48, erasing opening gains amid worries over a global trade war. With President Trump's 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum kicking in with no exemptions, the risk of a tit-for-tat tariff escalation is now more pronounced, says Vishnu Varathan, head of Macro Research for Asia ex-Japan at Mizuho Securities (Singapore). Among the worst performers on the benchmark index, Anta Sports falls 2.7%, Kuaishou Technology loses 2.2% and New Oriental Education & Technology Group is down 2.4%. Meanwhile, Li Auto rises 2.2% and Chow Tai Fook Jewellery adds 2.6%. The Hang Seng Tech Index is 0.8% lower at 5799.22. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0145 GMT - Chinese shares are lower in volatile early trade. "Global markets will remain on tenterhooks as sentiment remains fragile from rising tariff barriers," OCBC analysts say in a note. Resource stocks are leading the gains. China Shenhua Energy adds 0.45% and PetroChina is 0.5% higher. Software stocks are broadly lower, with Beijing Kingsoft Office Software down 0.2% and 360 Security Technology falling 1.1%. The benchmark Shanghai Composite Index is 0.1% lower at 3369,62, the Shenzhen Composite Index is down 0.5% and the ChiNext Price Index is off 0.2%. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0120 GMT - There are multiple reasons why Westpac is likely to underperform the broader Australian share market in 2025, according to the bank's bears at Morgan Stanley. They highlight eight reasons for their continued underweight rating, but point to three as the most important: margin trends, capital management and the lender's ongoing tech overhaul. The analysts point out in a note to clients that margins and capital both fell short of expectations in Westpac's first fiscal quarter, while investors are waiting on more detail about the tech overhaul benefits. Raising their expense estimates, the MS analysts cut their target price by 6.5% to A$27.30. Shares are down 1.3% at A$29.68. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0114 GMT - Singapore's FTSE Straits Times Index edges 0.1% lower to 3828.84 as investors digest mixed developments. Main theme appears to be relief after U.S. CPI data released Wednesday were slightly below consensus expectations, Commerzbank Research analysts say in a research report. However, there have also been fears of an escalation in the trade war, the analysts add, noting that the EU and Canada announced retaliatory tariffs against the U.S. overnight. Among the worst performers on the benchmark index, Yangzijiang Shipbuilding falls 2.6%, DFI Retail Group loses 1.75%, and Mapletree Industrial Trust is down 1.5%. Meanwhile, City Developments rises 3.6% and Hongkong Land adds 1.8%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0113 GMT - Malaysia's benchmark Kuala Lumpur Composite Index is up 0.4% at 1490.42 in early trade. While broad-based selling pressure has intensified on the bourse recently, sentiment is likely to improve today, supported by U.S. stocks' overnight rebound, analysts at Apex Securities write in a note. Bargain hunting activities could emerge but gains may be tepid given the absence of fresh leads, as global uncertainties continue to dominate, they add. Among the advancers, Gamuda rises 2.4%, YTL Power International gains 2.0% and Sunway is 1.7% higher. Meanwhile, Axiata Group and CelcomDigi each fall 1.7%, and CIMB Group is 0.6% lower. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0106 GMT - Continued cost-of-living pressures and economic uncertainty keep Macquarie analysts cautious on Premier Investments following its divestment of several clothing businesses. The analysts write in a note that the discretionary nature and relatively high price point of the conglomerate's remaining Smiggle and Peter Alexander brands mean they are exposed to the impact of inflation and elevated interest rates on consumers. They expect Premier to report first-half Ebit slightly above the midpoint of its A$160 million-A$165 million guidance range. Macquarie cuts its target price 26% to A$21.60 but keeps a neutral rating on the stock, which is down 1.7% at A$20.94. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0103 GMT - Rio Tinto's production and free cash flow are likely to accelerate faster than rival BHP's over the rest of this decade, Goldman Sachs analysts say in a note. Alongside valuation, that's why GS prefers Rio's stock over BHP, both of which it rates a buy, the analysts say. Despite both miners spending around US$10 billion-US$11 billion on capex annually, Rio's copper-equivalent production should rise about 20% by the end of the decade. BHP's output is expected to remain largely flat, with its major projects mostly longer dated, say the analysts.(rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0054 GMT - Macquarie analysts turn bearish on Australia's Audinate, citing potential headwinds including trade tariffs. Cutting their recommendation on the stock to underperform from neutral, the analysts tell clients in a note that they see no evidence for optimism on the audio-visual equipment maker. They point to softer economic growth, tariffs, weak market data, and continued discounting of adaptors to support their view. Management is responding soundly to softer demand but these market conditions are set to persist, they add. Macquarie cuts its target price 28% to A$6.30. Shares are down 0.7% at A$6.76. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0042 GMT - Orica's A$400 million share buyback confirms the explosives maker's near-term focus is on integrating recent acquisitions, and not pursuing more M&A, Macquarie analysts say in a note. Of Orica's integration efforts, "Terra is performing well following [the] successful Ground Probe acquisition whilst Cyanaco remains [a] work in progress," they say. The analysts were pleased to see the much-anticipated buyback eventuate, even if it was "a case of 'buy the rumor, sell the fact'" for investors, they say. After falling by 1.3% Wednesday, Orica's shares are up 1.3% in Sydney at A$16.895. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0036 GMT - The sell-off in U.S. shares this month has some investors starting to sniff returning value. The S&P 500 forward PE ratio fell from 25.3 in December to 20.7 now, says Harry Mamaysky, professor at Columbia Business School and cofounder of QuantStreet Capital. That's been partially driven by the market selloff and partly by increases in earnings estimates, he says. At the same time, the forward PE of European stocks and global stocks ex-U.S. are unchanged from December and trading at a lower PE multiple than the U.S., but the gap is now much lower, Mamaysky says. So on a relative valuation analysis basis, which also considers relative EPS growth rates and dividend yields, the U.S. market looks more compelling. Based on QuantStreet's tactical asset allocation model, the U.S. market is much more attractive than the global counterparts, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0022 GMT - South Korea's benchmark Kospi rises 0.6% to 2590.70, as shipbuilding and semiconductor stocks advance. Sentiment is supported by easing U.S. inflation fears after the Labor Department's report on softer-than-expected consumer prices for February. Retail and institutional investors are net buyers. Ship-engine maker HD-Hyundai Marine Engine climbs 8.0% after a new contract win. Hanwha Ocean rises 4.7%, leading a broad rally in shipbuilding stocks. Memory-chip maker SK Hynix adds 1.3%, tracking U.S. semiconductor stocks' rally overnight. USD/KRW is almost flat at 1,451.20 in Seoul onshore trading. South Korea's 10-year government bond yield is up 0.3 basis point at 2.763%. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

data.title: Delfi's Growth Likely to Be Dragged by Elevated Cocoa Prices — Market Talk
data.description: Delfi's growth in 2025 is likely to be dragged by elevated cocoa prices, UOB Kay Hian analysts say in a research report. They lower the stock's target price to S$0.82 from S$0.86, with an unchanged hold rating. Cocoa prices remain much higher than they have historically been owing to persistent supply issues, the analysts note. If the weather continues to be unfavorable in cocoa-producing regions, price increases are likely, they say. The brokerage cuts its 2025-2026 Ebitda margin forecasts for the chocolate confectionery product maker by 1-2 percentage points on expectations selling and distribution costs will remain high due to continued stiff competition. data.title: Crypto trading volume slumps, signaling market exhaustion: Analysis
data.description:
Crypto trading volumes and dwindling digital asset prices are flashing signs of trader exhaustion and potentially weaker market momentum, according to analysts. 

Crypto-wide trading volume has been dropping since it peaked in February amid dip-buying opportunities. According to CoinGecko data, daily trading volume hit its highest level this year in early February when it reached $440 billion. It has since sunk by 63% to $163 billion on March 12. 

Market data firm CoinMarketCap has slightly lower figures but they show the same trend — that volume peaked in 2025 in early March before falling back 52% to current levels.

Analytics firm Santiment said on X on March 13 that this decline in volume suggests that trader enthusiasm for the asset class is diminishing.

“When trading volume for major cryptocurrencies consistently drops, even during slight price recoveries, it typically points toward diminishing trader enthusiasm.”

Santiment added that trader behavior “indicates a mix of exhaustion, hopelessness, and capitulation” following further market capitalization declines over the past fortnight. 

Total market capitalization has declined almost 25% since the beginning of February, shrinking by $900 billion as the crypto market correction deepens. 

Those declines have accelerated over the past 10 days when markets have lost 15% as fears of a recession in the United States increased amid escalating global trade tensions.

Santiment stated that traders are becoming cautious, suggesting they might not believe that the current upward price movements will last. “Essentially, reduced trading activity reflects uncertainty, as fewer traders are convinced that buying at current levels will yield profitable outcomes,” the analysts added.

Weakening trading volume amid minor price bounces can serve as an “early warning sign of weakening market momentum,” Santiment reported, adding that without robust buying participation, price gains can quickly lose steam, “as there simply isn’t enough underlying support to sustain the upward trend.”

“This leads to the possibility that any rebound could be temporary, with prices vulnerable to another downturn.”

However, shrinking volume during minor rebounds isn’t necessarily a direct bearish signal, it said, adding that volume is a metric that measures participation from both retail and institutional traders and it needs to start rising before prices do.

 “To signal a healthier and more sustainable recovery, bulls generally will want to see both rising prices and rising volumes simultaneously.”

Crypto market capitalization is currently around $2.8 trillion, which is where it was this time last year before seven months of consolidation followed. 

Meanwhile, the Crypto Fear & Greed Index remains in “fear” territory, below 50, where it has been since Feb. 21.

data.title: Queensland Pacific Metals Returns to Profit in Fiscal H1 as Revenue Rises
data.description:
Queensland Pacific Metals reported Thursday earnings per diluted share of AU$0.0064 in the fiscal first half, compared with a loss of AU$0.0146 per diluted share a year earlier.

Revenue for the six months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was AU$73.5 million, compared with AU$39.4 million a year earlier.

The company's shares rose 2% in recent Thursday trade.

data.title: Market Chatter: Nomura Scaling Up Wealth Management Teams in Singapore, Dubai
data.description:
Nomura Holdings is scaling up its wealth management operations in Singapore and Dubai following an earlier-than-expected profit in its global business in 2024, Bloomberg News reported Wednesday, citing an official.

The company is moved to a larger office in Dubai, which can house up to 60 staff, twice the capacity of the previous location, said the publication, citing an interview with Ravi Raju, head of Nomura's international wealth business.

The firm is also increasing its workforce in Singapore, building on last year's staffing boost in Hong Kong, amid its diversification of revenue streams beyond investment banking, added the report.

The company aims to double its global assets under management to $60 billion and increase its relationship manager team by 50% within its current five-year plan, the report said.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Naver's Commission Adjustments Set to Boost Revenue, Earnings — Market Talk
data.description: Naver's commission adjustments, effective June 2, are set to boost revenue and earnings, Nomura analysts Angela Hong and Won Kang write in a note. The South Korean internet platform giant's new take-rate structure of reduced commissions can lead sellers to spend more on advertising, they say. The company has room to improve its e-commerce monetization given its low rates of commissions to sales, they note. They raise their earnings-per-share estimates for Naver by 4%-15% for 2025-2026. Nomura upgrades its rating on the stock to buy from neutral and raises its target price by 18% to KRW260,000. Shares are 1.4% higher at KRW217,000. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

data.title: Naver launches new AI-powered shopping app
data.description: Naver Corp. has launched a new AI-driven shopping application that provides personalized product recommendations, setting the stage for direct competition with Coupang Inc. On Wednesday, Naver announced that the Android version of its Naver Plus Store is now available, with an iOS version coming soon. The app is powered by Naver’s proprietary AI model, HyperCLOVA X, which rapidly analyzes vast amounts of product data and tailors recommendations based on user preferences, purchase history, browsing context, and intent to assist in shopping decisions. “With AI-powered recommendations, users can quickly discover products they are interested in, while sellers can enhance their marketing strategies by targeting high-potential buyers more effectively,” said a Naver official. Alongside the app launch, Naver has rebranded its delivery service from Naver Arrival Guarantee to Naver Delivery (N Delivery), introducing more refined delivery options, including same-day, next-day, Sunday, and scheduled deliveries to enhance logistics competitiveness. New features such as AI Shopping Guide and Discover have also been introduced. The AI Shopping Guide currently assists users in choosing laptops optimized for different needs, such as design, portability, office use, gaming, and studying. Naver plans to expand this feature to smartphones, refrigerators, and air conditioners. The Discover function presents short-form videos showcasing trending products and can be accessed through the app’s Discover tab at the bottom. Industry experts anticipate that Naver’s AI shopping app and enhanced delivery services will further intensify competition in the e-commerce sector. “The key question is whether Naver will take market share from competitors or expand the overall market itself,” said an industry insider.

data.title: Samsung Life Science Fund to invest in C2N Diagnostics
data.description: Samsung C&T Corp., a trading and investment unit under South Korea’s Samsung Group, announced on Thursday that it will invest $10 million in U.S.-based C2N Diagnostics LLC via its Samsung Life Science Fund. The company established the fund in collaboration with its affiliates Samsung Biologics Co., Samsung Bioepis Co., and Samsung Venture Investment Corp. According to Samsung C&T, C2N Diagnostics has developed a technology capable of precisely measuring and analyzing protein biomarkers that are present in trace amounts in the blood. The U.S. company has also developed a blood test for diagnosing Alzheimer’s disease based on the technology and has demonstrated its technological capabilities by participating in clinical trials for Alzheimer’s treatments and various central nervous system diseases. The Alzheimer’s blood test developed by C2N Diagnostics offers a safer and more cost-effective alternative to traditional amyloid PET-CT scans or cerebrospinal fluid tests, allowing for the precise measurement of various Alzheimer’s-related proteins, including amyloid-beta. The company plans to apply for U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approval for its Alzheimer’s blood test service within the year. “This investment will provide us with a strategic foothold for entry into this field,” Kim Jay-woo, executive vice president of Samsung C&T‘s Life Sciences division, said. C2N Diagnostics Chief Executive Officer Joel Braunstein added, “We have a differentiated competitive edge in the protein analysis sector through continuous technological innovation. This investment will help us strengthen our technological capabilities, attract top talent, and expand our services overseas.”

data.title: Samsung Electronics, SK hynix soar on overnight tech stock gains
data.description: Samsung Electronics Co. and SK hynix Inc. continued their upward momentum for the second day on Thursday. Shares of Samsung Electronics were trading at 55,300 won ($38.14) as of 10:29 a.m., up 0.73 percent from the previous day. SK hynix also surged 1.56 percent to 202,000 won as of 10:29 a.m. on Thursday. The stock gained to above the 200,000-won range for the first time in about two weeks. On Wednesday, Samsung Electronics closed 2.43 percent higher and SK hynix 5.91 percent higher. The rally comes after major tech stocks on the New York Stock Exchange gained on Wednesday. Shares of Nvidia Corp. rose 6.43 percent, Broadcom Inc. gained 2.18 percent, and Advanced Micro Devices Inc. soared 4.16 percent. Industry insiders noted that the faster-than-expected stabilization of the legacy semiconductor market is helping to drive stock prices higher. Kim Rok-ho, an analyst at Hana Securities Co., attributed the recovery in the NAND market to preemptive production cuts by Samsung Electronics, SK hynix, and Micron Technology Inc., as well as inventory reductions in smartphone channels. “The price increases by NAND makers suggest that Samsung and SK hynix now have room for upward adjustments,” Kim said. Expectations surrounding Nvidia’s upcoming artificial intelligence conference, GTC 2025, which kicks off on Monday (local time), are also seen as positively impacting semiconductor market supply and demand. Meanwhile, LS Corp. stock surged over 15 percent on Thursday morning, following news that Hoban Group had acquired a stake in LS, which is currently embroiled in a patent dispute with its subsidiaries. As of 10:33 a.m., LS shares were trading at 117,400 won, up 15.32 percent from the previous session. According to sources in the construction industry, Hoban Group recently purchased less than 3 percent of LS shares. While Hoban Group has stated that the purchase was a simple investment, speculation is rising that it could be a strategic move to gain a favorable position in an ongoing patent infringement lawsuit between the two groups. In August 2019, LS’s subsidiary, LS Cable & System Ltd., filed a lawsuit against Hoban Group’s subsidiary, Taihan Electric Wire Co., alleging patent infringement. The court ruled in favor of LS Cable on some claims in September 2022, but both parties have appealed, with the appeals court decision expected on Thursday.

data.title: Young Poong, MBK refute SMH ties
data.description: Young Poong and MBK Partners have dismissed Choi Yoon-beom’s claim that Young Poong’s voting rights were restricted due to a cross-shareholding structure between Young Poong and Korea Zinc as groundless. The two entities accused Choi, who is Korea Zinc’s chairman, of attempting to disrupt the upcoming annual general meeting (AGM) through baseless arguments. Young Poong and MBK issued a statement on Thursday that criticized Choi’s “deliberate strategy to derail the AGM by unjustly stripping the largest shareholder of its rightful voting rights.” The controversy arose after Sun Metals Corporation (SMC), a foreign subsidiary of Korea Zinc, transferred its 10.3 percent stake in Young Poong to its parent company, Sun Metals Holdings (SMH). Choi’s camp argues that this move establishes a cross-shareholding structure between Korea Zinc and Young Poong, restricting Young Poong’s voting rights. Young Poong and MBK rejected the claim, saying, “Choi’s argument is completely illogical. SMH and Young Poong have never had a cross-shareholding relationship, not even for a single second.” Under Article 369(3) of South Korea’s Commercial Act, a company (A) that directly or through a subsidiary holds more than 10 percent of another company’s (B) shares loses voting rights for any shares it holds in A. In this case, Korea Zinc is Company A, and Young Poong is Company B. However, Young Poong and MBK pointed out that as of the AGM record date (December 31st, 2024), SMH did not own any shares in Young Poong. Furthermore, Korea Zinc no longer holds any shares in Young Poong even after SMH acquired more than 10 percent of Young Poong, meaning the cross- shareholding rule does not apply. “Even on the day of Korea Zinc’s upcoming AGM in March, Young Poong will not be a shareholder of Korea Zinc. The claim that its voting rights should be restricted is baseless,” they emphasized. Young Poong and MBK further accused Choi of “manipulating company structures in a desperate attempt to obstruct the AGM at any cost.” They argued that Choi initially used SMC and later SMH to achieve this goal, revealing his intent to sabotage the shareholder meeting. “Now that fair competition over shareholding control is no longer in his favor, Choi is attempting to maintain his influence by unlawfully restricting the voting rights of the largest shareholder, which have been exercised without issue for 50 years,” the statement continued. “It is now evident that he has no regard for the independent interests of the company, the legitimate will of shareholders, or the legal principles governing corporate governance.” They concluded by saying, “We will ensure that Choi and his associates are held legally accountable and will deliver a decisive outcome at the AGM to restore stability to Korea Zinc’s board and recover shareholder value.”

data.title: Market Chatter: Samsung, SK Hynix Record Stronger China Sales in 2024
data.description:
The China sales of Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix surged in 2024 as Beijing's stimulus measures fueled semiconductor demand, Yonhap News reported Wednesday.

Samsung's exports to China jumped 53.9% to 64.9 trillion won while those of SK Hynix's Wuxi sales soared 64.3% to 13 trillion won. Both firms benefited from the increasing demand for memory chips, including LPDDR, NAND flash, and DRAM, the report said.

Shares of Samsung Electronics rose nearly 1% while those of SK Hynix increased 2% in recent trade.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: TrivarX Trial to Screen for Major Depressive Episode in Collaboration with US Department of Veteran Affairs
data.description:
TrivarX is set to conduct a clinical trial to identify a current major depressive episode using its single-channel electrocardiogram mental health screening algorithm in collaboration with the Greater Los Angeles Veterans Research and Education Foundation and the US Department of Veterans Affairs, according to a Wednesday Australian bourse filing.

It is working alongside the foundation and the department to submit a trial protocol to the department's institutional review board, which is pending approval.

It also negotiated a cooperative research and development agreement with the foundation and the department, which is expected to be signed following institutional review board approval.

The algorithm uses heart rate and heart-rate variability metrics to conduct sleep staging and screen for the episode. It will recruit 60 patients across multiple sites and the study is expected to be completed within 12 weeks from opening.

data.title: U.S. Less Equipped to Deal With a Recession — Market Talk
data.description: When Donald Trump became president for a second time the prospects of a U.S. recession in 2025 were small, but not non-trivial. Less than two months into his term that risk seems to me better than even-money, says Stephen Miller, investment strategy advisor at GSFM. And were it to come to pass, the U.S. authorities are less equipped to deal with a recession than they have been for some time, he adds. The current Atlanta Fed GDPNow estimate for current-quarter GDP is -2.4%, he notes. And even if the Fed may not be quite as constrained as feared, "sticky" inflation has robbed it of the flexibility to quickly cut rates, Miller says. The Trump agenda when it comes to things like tariffs and immigration risk a negative supply shock that makes inflation even stickier and growth more challenged. he says. And with a budget deficit approaching 7% of GDP, fiscal policy is close to maxed out, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0221 GMT - When Donald Trump became president for a second time the prospects of a U.S. recession in 2025 were small, but not non-trivial. Less than two months into his term that risk seems to me better than even-money, says Stephen Miller, investment strategy advisor at GSFM. And were it to come to pass, the U.S. authorities are less equipped to deal with a recession than they have been for some time, he adds. The current Atlanta Fed GDPNow estimate for current-quarter GDP is -2.4%, he notes. And even if the Fed may not be quite as constrained as feared, "sticky" inflation has robbed it of the flexibility to quickly cut rates, Miller says. The Trump agenda when it comes to things like tariffs and immigration risk a negative supply shock that makes inflation even stickier and growth more challenged. he says. And with a budget deficit approaching 7% of GDP, fiscal policy is close to maxed out, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0203 GMT - Asian currencies consolidate against the dollar in the morning session amid rising global trade tensions. "Elevated trade uncertainty has now become a negative for business confidence, for investment, and for growth," DBS Group Research's Chang Wei Liang says in a commentary. The outlook for U.S. economic exceptionalism could be fading, the FX & credit strategist says. The Dollar Index is consolidating around the mid-103s, getting little boost from escalating trade concerns, the strategist adds. USD/KRW edges 0.1% lower to 1,450.07; USD/SGD is little changed at 1.3326; USD/CNH is steady at 7.2396. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0136 GMT - U.S. tariffs directed at China are significant but surmountable impediments, says Elliot Clarke, head of international economics at Westpac. For the past 5 years, China has been broadening its export markets across Asia and Latin America, he says. For longer still, Chinese firms have also been expanding their production and logistic networks across Asia, particularly through southeast Asia, he adds. Not only is China able to redirect exports to non-U.S. markets, but can also produce for the U.S. outside of China to avoid tariffs, Clarke says. Even where Chinese firms cannot avoid U.S. tariffs, they are likely able to partly offset the cost of the tariffs through improved efficiency, he adds. Chinese exporters are therefore extremely well-placed to weather the economic uncertainty the U.S. is creating, Clarke says. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0035 GMT - JGBs edge higher in the morning Tokyo session, but market may be weighed by overnight price declines in U.S. Treasurys. JGBs and Treasurys tend to move in tandem. Meanwhile, some market participants could be wary of next week's BOJ meeting, which could signal an early rate increase, SMBC Nikko Securities' rates strategists say in a research report. However, even if the first round of this year's Spring wage negotiations are strong, risks of quickening inflation may not be substantially increased, so there seems to be a low probability that such as message would be sent, the strategists add. The JGB 10-year yield is down 0.5 bps at 1.515%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0014 GMT - Japanese stocks are higher as concerns about U.S. inflation ebb for now. Electronics and financial stocks are leading the gains. Advantest is up 5.3% and Rakuten Bank is 4.5% higher. USD/JPY is at 148.35, compared with 148.07 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments in Japanese monetary policy ahead of the Bank of Japan's policy meeting next week. The Nikkei Stock Average is up 1.1% at 37232.93. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0004 GMT - New Zealand investors have dropped the chance of Contact Energy securing regulatory clearance for its takeover of Manawa Energy to less than 40%, Forsyth Barr says. It believes Contact has offered a full price for Manawa, and it will take several years to deliver savings needed to justify the transaction cost. Analyst Andrew Harvey-Green doesn't expect Contact's share price to react much whatever New Zealand's Commerce Commission decides about the deal. "The situation is different for Manawa," Forsyth Barr says. "The passage of time means it is increasingly difficult to estimate the size of the market reaction to no deal." It thinks Manawa's share price could fall to around NZ$4.00 if the takeover is blocked, from NZ$4.88 currently. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2351 GMT - Japanese stocks may rise as concerns about U.S. inflation recede for now. Nikkei futures are up 0.9% at 37135 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 148.25, compared with 148.07 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Besides U.S. trade policy, investors are focusing on any developments in Japanese monetary policy ahead of the Bank of Japan's policy meeting next week. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.1% to 36819.09 on Wednesday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

2157 GMT - Bank of Canada senior officials did discuss leaving its policy rate unchanged but opted for a quarter-point cut because of polling it conducted suggesting business and consumer confidence had turned sour due to tariff threats, Gov. Tiff Macklem tells Reuters. He says one scenario contemplated was keeping the policy rate at 3%. "The argument for staying at 3% was growth has been stronger than we expected," Macklem tells Reuters. During his press conference, Macklem said there was no serious thought given to a half-point cut. The fact some senior policymakers talked about leaving rates unchanged might help explain BOC's communications — which was relatively strident, or hawkish, about tariff-fueled inflation risks. The decision has traders and economists uncertain about an another cut in April.(paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

2009 GMT - Mexico's industrial production likely picked up in January after a decline in December, while remaining below year-earlier levels for a sixth consecutive month amid weakness in mining and construction. Industrial production is expected to have risen 0.4% seasonally adjusted from December, according to a Wall Street Journal survey of analysts. Production is expected to be down 1.7% unadjusted from January of 2024. Statistics institute Inegi is scheduled to report industrial production on Thursday. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1941 GMT - Treasury yields rise as inflation cools but remain above the Fed's target, amid concerns that tariffs could keep prices hot in coming months. February PPI wholesale price index is due tomorrow. The EPA announces measures to reduce red tape, promising they will unleash investment and reduce energy and other costs. A 10-year Treasury auction is met with solid demand, with a bid-to-cover ratio of 2.59, a little above the 12-month average. Jobless claims tomorrow are expected to rise slightly, in a WSJ survey. The 10-year gains 0.029 percentage point to 4.315% and the two-year rises 0.055 p.p. to 3.994%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1902 GMT - The recent turbulence in financial markets is making corporate bonds look cheaper relative to Treasurys, but still not enough to compensate for the increased risk stemming from policy uncertainty tied to President Trump, Madison Investments' Mike Sanders says. The ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread has widened to 3.2% as of yesterday, from 2.6% in late January, according to FRED data. "We have been reducing some relative credit risk in our portfolios," Sanders says. He is buying more mortgages and owns "a good amount" of Treasurys. "We are just kind of watching the market and seeing if there is an opportunity to add either mortgages or credit." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1834 GMT - Royal Bank of Canada reckons there won't be a race to the bottom for Canadian interest rates beyond what it has already expected this year projecting the central bank's policy rate will decline to 2.25% around midyear. That's despite the turmoil the trade war with the U.S. is expected to bring. Economist Claire Fan says that after the latest quarter-point cut to 2.75% data releases will going forward be monitored closed for signs weaker sentiment is reflected in actual spending, GDP and labor numbers. Data also will be watched for any evidence the uptick in inflation in recent months is persisting and pushing up expectations, and for any fiscal response from Ottawa to addressing trade disruptions. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

data.title: Couche-Tard goes on charm offensive for Japan's Seven & i after board shake-up
data.description:
Key points:
  • Canadian firm to hold first press conference in Tokyo since announcing bid last August
  • Couche-Tard says it is confident it can overcome antitrust concerns
  • The $47 billion deal, if completed, would be Japan's biggest-ever foreign buyout

Alimentation Couche-Tard is seeking to win over the Japanese public for its $47 billion bid for Seven & i on Thursday and address antitrust concerns that have emerged as a major stumbling block in its quest to buy the 7-Eleven owner.

Canada's Couche-Tard, which owns the Circle-K convenience store chain, has been pursuing Seven & i for months even as it has received a frosty reception from the Japanese retail giant, in what would be Japan's largest-ever foreign buyout if the deal is completed.

Executives from Couche-Tard are holding their first press conference in Tokyo on Thursday, months after announcing a bid for Seven & i in August and highlighting the Canadian company's recent push to woo a Japanese public sceptical of a foreign takeover.

The event comes a day after two of Seven & i's independent directors resigned from the board, a development that one shareholder, U.S.-based Artisan Partners said was a "sign of dysfunction" at Seven & i. Artisan has repeatedly called on the Japanese company to engage more actively with Couche-Tard.

Seven & i has repeatedly said that potential antitrust issues in the United States would make the proposed takeover difficult.

Couche-Tard said this week it was confident there was a "clear path" to overcome any U.S. regulatory hurdles and expressed frustration at the 7-Eleven owner's "limited engagement."

Couche-Tard also said it had been working with Seven & i on a plan to divest some of their stores in the United States.

Seven & i's newly appointed CEO Stephen Dacus has reiterated that significant regulatory hurdles stand in the way of a deal. The firms are the top two players in the U.S. convenience store market, with about 20,000 locations between them.

Couche-Tard has offered to pay $18.19 per share in Seven & i, representing a roughly 23% premium over the Japanese company's share price of 2,196 yen ($14.82) on Thursday.

ANTITRUST ISSUES

Couche-Tard management's trip to Tokyo and the engagement with Seven & i on antitrust concerns underscore the lengths to which dealmakers would go to ensure deal certainty amid U.S. regulatory scrutiny.

To engage in detailed divestment discussions for antitrust purposes before a deal is agreed or any confidentiality agreement is signed is uncommon in transactions, deal advisers said.

"I can't say I've seen a case where prior to a merger agreement being executed the entire divestiture package and buyer were set in stone and baked into the merger agreement," said Kathy O'Neill, a partner at law firm Fried Frank.

But she said working on a divestiture package before a merger agreement was reached would help to potentially reduce the risk of surprise and time and effort put into chasing a deal.

Tim Cornell, a litigation partner and member of the Debevoise & Plimpton's Antitrust Group, agreed the airing of antitrust concerns before a deal was announced was not typical.

"In certain circumstances, buyers will test the waters with regards to a divestiture package especially where they've identified that's what is needed," he said.

Couche-Tard sweetened its offer in October and has said it remained committed to the deal, after a competing $58 billion management buyout proposed by Seven & i's founding family failed to materialise.

data.title: China's YTO Express falls as Alibaba's affiliate plans stake cut
data.description:
** Shares of China's courier delivery services group YTO Express Group Co Ltd fall 5% to 13.11 yuan, lowest since March 12, 2024

** Stock set for the biggest one-day decline since October 9, 2024

** Alibaba affiliate Hangzhou Haoyue Enterprise Management plans to unload up to 2% stake in YTO Express within three months

** Alibaba's Hong Kong shares fall 2.3%

** The blue-chip CSI 300 Index gains 0.2%, the Shanghai Composite Index adds 0.1%

** YTO stock down 6.8% YTD
